post.title: Getting out of my comfort zone
post.body:
Yeah, I was thinking about the previous video where I talked about getting out of my comfort zone. I figured I might as well talk about it right now because it perfectly connects with a lot of the things I value.

When I was in quarantine, things got to the point where I had basically no friends. I literally went on Google and searched, "How do I make friends?" That sent me down a huge rabbit hole of videos and articles about making friends.

At some point, I came across a YouTube video. It didn't have many views—I don't even know if it's still up—but it was this guy who made an entire exposure therapy plan.

The first step was documenting how I felt. Then, every day, I would intentionally put myself into an uncomfortable social situation, like talking to someone new. Afterward, I would document how I felt again. There were other parts to it, too, but that was the main idea.

Around that time, I had also moved to a new school. There were obviously other factors involved, but I was actually excited because it meant I had a fresh start. I didn't have to worry about people judging me based on who I used to be. No one knew me as "the quiet kid."

I had a clean slate.

My biggest goal was simply to make friends.

Using that exposure therapy plan, my goal was to start one conversation—or even just join one conversation—every single day.

It was honestly that bad. Back then, even joining a conversation felt difficult.

So I pushed myself.

At first, I might have only contributed a single sentence to a conversation, and that alone felt like a win.

Little by little, though, I became more comfortable. Over time, it genuinely helped me become much more confident talking to people.

I think I still have those journal entries somewhere. They documented exactly how I felt before and after each day.

I even recorded myself while playing games like Rocket League or Genshin Impact. I'd just talk to myself, exactly like I'm doing now, and reflect on how the day went.

I think I made it to around Day 30—maybe Day 32.

After about a month, I finally felt like I didn't need those recordings anymore because I had found people I could actually talk to. I finally felt like I had friends.

It was incredibly rewarding.

Granted, that friend group didn't end up lasting. Four years later, things didn't really work out.

But because of those friendships, I ended up meeting even more people.

One of those friendships eventually led me to my current friend group through tennis.

Then there were clubs, like the Beat Saber Club.

Those people eventually became my day ones.

They're real.

Honestly, I'm incredibly grateful to have them.

They're probably one of the biggest reasons why I'm so content with life now and why I don't really struggle with loneliness anymore.

I can be my full, authentic self around them.

It's great.

So, if you're listening and you know who you are, thank you.

Anyway, before I get into my friendship video, I wanted to talk about another situation where stepping outside my comfort zone completely changed my life.

One of my biggest fears growing up was doing things alone.

I always wanted someone to be with me whenever I went somewhere, especially social events or concerts.

Even now, I notice people saying they won't go to a concert because they don't have anyone to go with.

Nowadays, though, my mindset is different.

If you really want to see an artist, just go by yourself.

One experience completely changed how I looked at that.

One day, Laufey posted an Instagram story showing where she was. I realized she was only about thirty minutes away from where I lived. She was near the venue where she was performing later that night.

The concert itself was completely sold out.

But she wasn't inside the venue yet.

I remember thinking, "What if I just go?"

So I drove over by myself and waited near the venue's back entrance.

I honestly don't know what got into me that day, but I decided I was going to step outside my comfort zone.

When I got there, there were already a few people waiting.

I built up the courage to talk to them, and we all started chatting about Laufey.

Then, sure enough, she walked by.

I got to meet her, talk to her, and take a picture with her.

For some reason, I never asked her to sign anything.

Looking back, I definitely should have gotten an autograph too.

But honestly, I was just happy that I got to meet her, especially considering how huge she is now.

The crazy part happened afterward.

A staff member came outside and told everyone waiting that if we stayed, there might be extra tickets available.

So we waited.

The concert had completely sold out, and resale tickets were going for around $400.

Somehow, we ended up getting front-row balcony tickets for only $30.

Those seats would've probably been worth close to $1,000 on resale.

It was incredible.

That was during her Bewitched tour, back when "From the Start" had just started blowing up but she was still performing at relatively small venues.

Even better, I ended up making a bunch of new friends while waiting outside.

Unfortunately, I don't really keep in touch with most of them anymore, but the fact that I was able to make friends there at all was amazing.

That experience is another reason why I'm so much more comfortable doing things alone now.

I became comfortable because I repeatedly exposed myself to uncomfortable situations.

There's actually a psychology concept called the mere exposure effect.

The more you're exposed to something, the more comfortable or familiar it starts to feel.

From everything I've seen, that's also one of the best ways to overcome fear.

You can correct me if I'm wrong, but from what I've learned, that's basically how you overcome most fears.

You face them.

That's why "face your fears" is such a common saying.

It's really just repeated exposure.

The fear might never disappear completely, but it becomes much smaller.

I still have social anxiety sometimes.

It's definitely not gone.

But it's so much better than it used to be.

And that's exactly why I value getting out of my comfort zone so highly.
